description:
You are playing the role of a penetration tester who has been
    handed a small set of internal hosts to scan.  
    A working directory already exists at  

        /home/user/pen_test  

    containing a single text file named  

        /home/user/pen_test/targets.txt  

    with one IPv4 address per line.

    Your mission is to build a fully-automated “mini
    vulnerability-pipeline” that does all of the following while
    supporting basic error-recovery, and then prove that it worked by
    leaving very specific artefacts on disk.

    1.  Read every line in /home/user/pen_test/targets.txt.
    2.  For each target do a TCP scan limited to the first 1024 ports.
        •  If the program nmap is available in PATH, use it.  
        •  If nmap is not available, fall back to a “mock scan”
           that simply creates an output file containing the single
           line

               Host: <IP>, Port:80, State:open, Service:http, Protocol:tcp

           (replace <IP> by the currently processed address).
    3.  Place the raw output of each individual scan in

            /home/user/pen_test/scans/<IP>.nmap

        (one file per host).
    4.  When a scan command exits with a non-zero exit-code, append a
        structured line to the error log

            /home/user/pen_test/logs/errors.log

        using the exact format

            [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS] ERROR <IP> <exit-code>: <reason>

        •  <reason> must be the first line of stderr if such output
          exists, otherwise “no stderr”.
        •  Even if errors happen, continue scanning the remaining
          hosts.
    5.  After all scans finish, consolidate the findings into the CSV

            /home/user/pen_test/summary/report.csv

        with this precise header row:

            IP,Port,State,Service,Protocol,Notes

        Then, for every discovered open port (real or mocked) append a
        row with the five primary columns filled and leave the sixth
        “Notes” column blank for now.  
        CSV values must be comma-separated with no extra spaces.
    6.  Keep an overall run-log in

            /home/user/pen_test/logs/run.log

        where each line follows the pattern

            [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS] <STAGE> <IP> <message>

        Valid <STAGE> tokens are START, FINISH, SKIP, ERROR.  
        •  START before a scan actually begins.  
        •  FINISH after a scan succeeds.  
        •  SKIP if the address line is blank or commented out
          (targets.txt might contain “#” comments).  
        •  ERROR if the scan command fails (this must match the entry
          placed in errors.log).

    7.  Everything you create must remain inside the existing directory
        tree below /home/user/pen_test.  Do not write anywhere else.
        Make sure all newly created files are world-readable.

    What the automated checker will look for
    ----------------------------------------
    1.  Directory structure:

            /home/user/pen_test/scans
            /home/user/pen_test/logs
            /home/user/pen_test/summary

    2.  For every IP found in targets.txt:
        •  a file /home/user/pen_test/scans/<IP>.nmap must exist.
        •  at least one corresponding line must appear in
          /home/user/pen_test/summary/report.csv.

    3.  report.csv must begin with the exact header stated above.

    4.  The timestamp format in every log file is strictly
        “YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS” (24-hour clock, zero-padded).

    5.  errors.log should exist even if it ends up empty (0 bytes).

    6.  run.log must contain a START and FINISH (or ERROR) pair for
        every non-skipped IP.

    When you are done, the final directory tree will look like this
    (the IP addresses below are just examples; the checker will use the
    real values from targets.txt):

        /home/user/pen_test/
        ├── logs/
        │   ├── errors.log
        │   └── run.log
        ├── scans/
        │   ├── 192.168.0.10.nmap
        │   ├── 192.168.0.11.nmap
        │   └── 192.168.0.12.nmap
        └── summary/
            └── report.csv

    Create any helper scripts you need (bash, Python, etc.) inside
    /home/user/pen_test; name is up to you.  The automated judge will
    run only the *outputs* described above, so make sure they match the
    required structure and exact formats.
truth:
<!--
        The starting file /home/user/pen_test/targets.txt already exists
        before the task begins and contains these three lines EXACTLY:

            192.168.0.10
            192.168.0.11
            192.168.0.12

        There are no comment or blank lines in the initial file, so the
        agent must scan all three addresses.

        The judge will verify that:
        --------------------------------
        1. /home/user/pen_test/scans/192.168.0.10.nmap
           /home/user/pen_test/scans/192.168.0.11.nmap
           /home/user/pen_test/scans/192.168.0.12.nmap
           all exist and contain the single line
               Host: <IP>, Port:80, State:open, Service:http, Protocol:tcp
           (with the correct IP substituted).
           The ground-truth values for those lines are:
               Host: 192.168.0.10, Port:80, State:open, Service:http, Protocol:tcp
               Host: 192.168.0.11, Port:80, State:open, Service:http, Protocol:tcp
               Host: 192.168.0.12, Port:80, State:open, Service:http, Protocol:tcp

        2. /home/user/pen_test/summary/report.csv must have EXACTLY four lines:
               IP,Port,State,Service,Protocol,Notes
               192.168.0.10,80,open,http,tcp,
               192.168.0.11,80,open,http,tcp,
               192.168.0.12,80,open,http,tcp,

        3. /home/user/pen_test/logs/errors.log is present and empty.

        4. /home/user/pen_test/logs/run.log contains six lines whose
           <STAGE> parts are in this exact sequence:
               START 192.168.0.10
               FINISH 192.168.0.10
               START 192.168.0.11
               FINISH 192.168.0.11
               START 192.168.0.12
               FINISH 192.168.0.12
           and whose timestamps satisfy the required format.  Because we
           cannot predict exactly when the agent will run, the judge only
           checks that every line starts with “[YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS]”.

        5. All files are world-readable (mode 0644 or wider).

        The agent does NOT need to install or run the real nmap; the
        expected ground-truth purposely assumes the mock-scan path so
        that the test remains deterministic.
    -->
name: task_000000_0dbc0d6a
